GenAI Beyond the Basics: Advanced Concepts for Actuaries

Simon Hatzesberger (simon.hatzesberger@gmail.com)

4 December 2025

Table of Contents

1. Introduction and Key Takeaways

2. Using LLMs via API

3. Structured Outputs
   3.1 Concept and Purpose
   3.2 Use Case: Structured Data Extraction from Policy Documents
   3.3 Further Applications and Resources

4. Function Calling
   4.1 Concept and Purpose
   4.2 Use Case: Mortality Data Retrieval from Database via Function Calling
   4.3 Further Applications and Resources

5. Fine‑Tuning
   5.1 Concept and Purpose
   5.2 Use Case: Improving Car Damage Classification by Fine-Tuning Vision-Enabled LLMs
   5.3 Further Applications and Resources

6. Retrieval‑Augmented Generation
   6.1 Concept and Purpose
   6.2 Use Case: Extracting KPIs from Annual Reports with RAG
   6.3 Further Applications and Resources

7. Outlook
   7.1 Combining GenAI Building Blocks: Real-Time Hail-Claim Assessment
   7.2 Multi-Agent Systems: From Single Models to Collaborative AI Agents

1. Introduction and Key Takeaways

This Jupyter Notebook introduces advanced concepts and techniques in Generative AI (GenAI) that go well beyond a simple chat interface such as ChatGPT. We will explore how actuaries can integrate and extend modern Large Language Model (LLM) capabilities through Fine‑Tuning, Structured Outputs, Retrieval‑Augmented Generation (RAG), and Function Calling. These techniques expand what an LLM can do, allow you to tailor it to specialised actuarial tasks, and help you embed GenAI seamlessly into production workflows.

Because GenAI is rapidly reshaping content creation, automation, and decision‑support, a solid grasp of the tools "under the hood" has become indispensable for actuarial work. This notebook targets actuaries and data scientists who already have basic Python skills and want to deepen their technical understanding of GenAI's programmatic power.

Core Techniques Covered¶

  • Structured Outputs — Enforce responses to be in predefined JSON formats for downstream processing.
  • Function Calling — Let the model trigger deterministic functions (e.g. database queries, calculations) so that "thinking" and "doing" work hand in hand.
  • Fine‑Tuning — Refine a pretrained model with domain‑specific data to improve accuracy and relevance.
  • Retrieval‑Augmented Generation — Inject external knowledge into model queries for richer, contextual responses.

Key Takeaways Upon Completing This Notebook¶

  • You will know how to call OpenAI's LLMs via API from Python and integrate them into actuarial workflows.
  • You will be able to determine when Structured Outputs, Function Calling, Fine‑Tuning, RAG, or a combination thereof offers the most effective solution to a problem.
  • You will experience hands‑on actuarial use cases and receive reusable, adaptable code snippets for each technique.
  • You will leave with practical pointers to further applications, learning resources, and best‑practice guidelines.

The notebook is organised as follows:
Section 2 (Using LLMs via API) covers API fundamentals and shows how to send your first prompts. Sections 3 to 6 dive deeply into each core technique (Structured Outputs, Function Calling, Fine‑Tuning, and Retrieval‑Augmented Generation). Every section follows the same rhythm: Concept and Purpose → Use Case → Further Applications and Resources. Finally, Section 7 (Outlook) looks ahead to upcoming GenAI trends such as LLM-based agents.

2. Using LLMs via API

Most actuaries first encounter Generative AI (GenAI) through web‑based chat interfaces such as ChatGPT – the classic browser window is shown in the screenshot below.


ChatGPT accessed through its standard web interface.
[Source: chatgpt.com]

When we access Large Language Models programmatically via API, we unlock capabilities that go far beyond the web chat:

  • Seamless integration — call GenAI directly from actuarial (Python) workflows.

  • Massive scale — issue thousands of requests concurrently without throttling your own productivity.

  • Full conversational control — tune instructions, temperature, and other parameters to steer tone, depth, and format of the response.

  • Data synergy — combine the model's reasoning with in‑house datasets, downstream code, or external knowledge bases.

Broader topics – each worthy of its own deep‑dive – that fall outside the scope of this notebook are as follows:

  • Mathematical/Technical Foundations of LLMs — tokenisation, transformer blocks, training objectives, and optimisation.

  • Governance and compliance considerations for enterprise use of GenAI.

  • Deep‑dive prompt‑engineering theory (beyond the practical tips we provide on the way).

  • Production deployment & MLOps topics such as monitoring, model versioning, and automation of updates.

For those areas, please consult specialized courses or official documentation—our priority here is to equip you with practical OpenAI API skills as efficiently as possible.

Note

Everything you see in this notebook is demonstrated with OpenAI's models, yet the underlying ideas – authentication, prompt design, Structured Outputs, Function Calling, Fine-Tuning, Retrieval‑Augmented Generation – apply almost one‑to‑one to other leading providers such as Anthropic Claude, Mistral, Google Gemini or open‑source models served through an API.

Python environment¶

All cells below assume a local or cloud Python environment in which the packages listed in the file requirements.txt have been installed.
If you have not done so yet, run

pip install -r requirements.txt

(or use your favourite environment manager such as pipx, conda, poetry or virtualenv) before you execute the rest of the notebook.

In [1]:
# Uncomment the next line if you need to (re)install the packages directly
# %pip install -r requirements.txt

First, we load all necessary libraries.

In [2]:
# Standard library imports
import os
import logging
import json
import base64
import re
import sqlite3
from urllib.parse import urlparse, unquote

# Typing utilities
from typing import List, Optional, Literal

# OpenAI client
from openai import OpenAI

# Numerical and tabular data handling
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Configure pandas display options for enhanced readability
pd.set_option('display.expand_frame_repr', False)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 3000)
pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)

# Plotting
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Machine learning metrics and model selection
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

# Progress bars
from tqdm import tqdm

# HTTP requests
import requests

# PDF and image processing
import fitz
from PIL import Image

# Data validation
from pydantic import BaseModel

# Local data and API helpers
import kagglehub

Authenticating with the OpenAI API¶

Every request to the OpenAI back‑end must be signed with a secret API key.
You can create one in your OpenAI dashboard and revoke it at any time.

There are two common ways to make the key available to your code:

  1. Environment variable (recommended for production)
    export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
  2. Text file in the project folder that is never committed to version control (e.g. openai_api_key.txt).

The helper function below transparently supports both variants.

In [3]:
def load_openai_api_key(file_path: str = "openai_api_key.txt", env_var: str = "OPENAI_API_KEY") -> str:
    """Return an OpenAI API key either from an environment variable or a local file."""
    key = os.getenv(env_var)
    if key:
        return key.strip()
    if os.path.exists(file_path):
        with open(file_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            return f.read().strip()
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "No OpenAI API key found. Set the environment variable '{}' or create the file '{}'".format(env_var, file_path)
    )

# Initialise the global OpenAI client once
api_key = load_openai_api_key()
client = OpenAI(api_key=api_key)

Below is a minimal Python example demonstrating how to call an OpenAI LLM via the API. We use the client.responses.create() method to:

  1. Specify which model to invoke (gpt-4.1-mini),
  2. Pass in a simple actuarial prompt, and
  3. Print out the model's reply.
In [4]:
# Define the default model to be used for OpenAI API calls
MODEL_DEFAULT = "gpt-4.1-mini"

response = client.responses.create(
    model=MODEL_DEFAULT,
    input="In one sentence, explain how the loss ratio is calculated for an auto-insurance portfolio."
)
print(response.output_text)

The loss ratio for an auto-insurance portfolio is calculated by dividing the total incurred losses (claims paid plus loss reserves) by the total earned premiums over a specific period.

This API call pattern will reappear throughout the notebook—often extended with the additional code required for each GenAI technique under discussion.

Note

Each API call incurs a charge based on the number of tokens processed (input + output), where one token is roughly ¾ of a word. See OpenAI’s pricing page for current rates. Ensure your account balance is positive before making requests to avoid failed calls.

Instructions matter – an actuarial example¶

The instructions parameter functions like the system message at the start of a conversation, defining the assistant’s tone, depth, and focus. In this example, we ask the model:

What is the Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) and why is it important?

in two ways:

  1. Default prompt (no special instructions)
  2. Guided role prompt that frames the assistant as "a senior actuary" who must explain the SCR in plain English for a new analyst, keep it brief, and end with one practical takeaway.
In [5]:
question = "What is the Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) and why is it important?"

# Variant 1 – default prompt
response_plain = client.responses.create(
    model=MODEL_DEFAULT,
    input=question
)

# Variant 2 – with simple role instructions
role_instructions = (
    "You are a senior actuary. Explain the SCR in plain English for a new analyst, "
    "keep it brief, and end with one practical takeaway."
)
response_role = client.responses.create(
    model=MODEL_DEFAULT,
    input=question,
    instructions=role_instructions
)

print("——— Without instructions ———\n", response_plain.output_text)
print("\n——— With instructions ———\n", response_role.output_text)

——— Without instructions ———
 The **Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR)** is a key regulatory metric used primarily in the insurance industry, especially under the **Solvency II Directive** in the European Union. It represents the amount of capital that an insurance company must hold to ensure that it can meet its obligations over the next 12 months with a certain confidence level, typically 99.5%.

### Detailed Explanation:

- **Definition:**  
  The SCR is the minimum capital an insurer needs to cover its risk exposures, including underwriting risk, market risk, credit risk, and operational risk. It reflects the capital requirement to withstand significant losses in adverse scenarios.

- **Purpose and Importance:**  
  - **Policyholder Protection:** The SCR ensures that insurers have enough financial resources to pay claims, providing security and confidence to policyholders.  
  - **Risk Sensitivity:** The SCR calculation considers the specific risk profile of the company, meaning it is risk-based rather than a fixed amount, promoting better risk management and financial stability.  
  - **Regulatory Compliance:** Meeting the SCR is mandatory under regulations like Solvency II. Failure to meet SCR requirements can trigger supervisory intervention or restrictions on the insurer's activities.  
  - **Market Confidence:** Adequate capital reserves bolster the confidence of investors, customers, and regulators, enhancing the insurer’s reputation and stability in the financial markets.  
  - **Encourages Better Risk Management:** By linking capital requirements to risk profiles, it encourages insurers to identify, measure, and manage risks more effectively.

### Summary:
The **Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR)** is the amount of capital an insurer must hold to remain solvent with a 99.5% confidence over one year, reflecting its risk profile. It is crucial for protecting policyholders, ensuring regulatory compliance, maintaining market stability, and promoting prudent risk management in the insurance sector.

——— With instructions ———
 The Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) is the amount of money an insurance company needs to hold to stay financially strong and cover its risks over the next year with high confidence. It acts like a safety cushion to ensure the company can pay claims even in tough situations, such as big losses or unexpected events.

Practically, the SCR helps protect policyholders by making sure the insurer won't run out of money when they need to pay. For you as an analyst, always remember: the SCR is a key measure of the company’s financial health and risk exposure.

Another advantage of the instructions parameter is that it enables you to establish a consistent task definition across multiple calls, while supplying different inputs – such as text, image representations, or numeric data – via the input parameter (see, for instance, the use case in Subsection 5.2).

3. Structured Outputs

3.1 Concept and Purpose

LLMs are designed to produce human-readable prose, yet many actuarial workflows require machine-readable data that drops straight into code, spreadsheets, or databases. The concept of Structured Outputs solves this by enforcing the model to return a response that follows a predefined JSON schema rather than free-form text. When the LLM reliably fills named fields with well-specified data types, the result can be parsed deterministically – removing the need for fragile regex parsers and ad-hoc clean-up scripts.

Note

OpenAI's text_format parameter – successor to the earlier response_format parameter – is the recommended way to apply Structured Outputs. Combined with Pydantic (or a comparable schema library), it ensures end-to-end adherence to your JSON schema. The same workflow applies to other providers: declare the schema, embed its definition in the prompt, and instruct the model to honour it.

Why It Matters for Actuaries¶

  • Repeatability – pricing, reserving, and reporting pipelines receive the same field names and formats every run.
  • Interoperability – JSON can be consumed by spreadsheets, BI dashboards, or actuarial software (e.g., Prophet, ResQ) with no extra transformation layer.
  • Expressiveness – nested objects and optional fields capture complex insurance concepts such as multi-vehicle policies, layered treaties, or cash-flow waterfalls.

How Structured Outputs Work¶

  1. Define the schema – create a Pydantic model or JSON schema that represents the desired output.
  2. Send prompt + schema – include the schema (or a textual description of it) in the prompt and set text_format.
  3. LLM validation – the model fills in the fields and self-checks before responding.
  4. Parse and use – your application loads the JSON into a data class and feeds it straight into downstream code.

3.2 Use Case: Structured Data Extraction from Policy Documents

In this use case we ask an LLM to analyse a synthetic automobile insurance policy from the open-access RISCBAC corpus — a dataset of 10 000 bilingual (FR/EN) contracts generated from the Québec standard form.

A RISCBAC auto policy embeds a variety of structured facts that we'll extract in this notebook, for example:

  • Policy and endorsement numbers
  • Insured's personal details (name, address, date of birth)
  • Vehicle information (VIN, make, model, year)
  • Coverage sections with limits and deductibles
  • Premium amounts by coverage type
  • Policy effective and expiry dates

Our task is to extract specified data fields from the narrative policy and format them as a machine-readable JSON object, which in a real-world workflow could then be consumed by downstream code.

The next cell simply loads the first English contract from riscbac_en_first10.jsonl and prints the raw text content.

In [6]:
# Set the desired row number (1-based index)
row_number = 1  # Change this to the row (1-10) you want

# Path to your JSONL file
file_path = "riscbac_en_first10.jsonl"

# Read and parse the JSONL file
with open(file_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

data = json.loads(lines[row_number - 1])  # Convert to 0-based index
text_content = data.get("text", "")

print(text_content)

	1. Log in to the Client Centre from your phone at assureur.ca/clientcentre.
	2. In the Home, auto and leisure vehicles section, click on ''Get an insurance certificate'', then''Add to Apple Wallet''.
You will have to update your certificates at each renewal of, or change to, your insurance policy by downloading it again to the Apple Wallet application.
Assureur cannot guarantee that the electronic version of the certificates will be accepted by all police forces. Make sure you always have a paper version on hand.

CERTIFICAT D'ASSURANCE POUR LOCATION DE VOITURE
(Valide au Canada et aux États-Unis d'Amérique seulement)
Application particulière de l'avenant F.A.Q. 27 : Responsabilité civile du fait de dommages causés à des véhicules dont l'assuré désigné n'est pas propriétaire (incluant les véhicules fournis par un employeur).
En cas de vol ou d'endommagement d'un véhicule de location, Assureur s'engage à rembourser, à l'Assuré ou au propriétaire du véhicule, le montant des dommages réellement encourus et dont l'Assuré ou son conjoint s'est rendu responsable en vertu d'un contrat de location, le tout jusqu'à concurrence de : 75 000 $

CAR RENTAL INSURANCE CERTIFICATE
(Valid only in Canada and the United States of America)
Speciﬁc application of Q.E.F. endorsement B 27 : Civil liability resulting from damage caused to vehicles of which named insured is not owner (including vehicles provided by an employer).
In the event of theft or damage to a rental vehicle, Assureur agrees to refund the Insured or the owner of the vehicle, the amount of damage actually incurred and for which the Insured or his/her spouse assumes liability under a rental contract, in all up to: $75,000
CERTIFICAT D'ASSURANCE RESPONSABILITÉ AUTOMOBILE
Le présent certiﬁcat est assujetti aux dispositions et conditions de la police d'assurance automobile de l'Assureur.
Ce certiﬁcat atteste que la personne susnommée est assurée contre la responsabilité pour blessures et dommages aux biens découlant de l'usage du véhicule ci-décrit, conformément aux limites minimales exigées par les lois d'assurance en vigueur dans chacune des provinces canadiennes.
AVERTISSEMENT - Quiconque émet ou présente un tel certiﬁcat comme preuve d'une police d'assurance responsabilité qui effectivement n'est pas en vigueur, est coupable d'une infraction passible d'une forte amende et/ou d'emprisonnement et suspension de son permis.
Tout sinistre devrait être rapporté immédiatement.

CANADA INTER-PROVINCE MOTOR VEHICLE LIABILITY INSURANCE CARD
This certiﬁcate is subject to the terms and conditions of the Insurers standard automobile policy.
This certiﬁes that the party named herein is insured against liability for bodily injury and property damage by reason of the operation of the motor vehicle described herein, for an amount not less than the statutory minimum requirements of every province of Canada.
WARNING - Any person who issues or presents such a certiﬁcate as proof of insurance when no policy exists is guilty of fraud and liable to a heavy ﬁ ne and/or imprisonment. His driving permit may also be suspended.
All loss should be reported immediately.

CERTIFICAT D'ASSURANCE RESPONSABILITÉ AUTOMOBILE
Le présent certiﬁcat est assujetti aux dispositions et conditions de la police d'assurance automobile de l'Assureur.
Ce certiﬁcat atteste que la personne susnommée est assurée contre la responsabilité pour blessures et dommages aux biens découlant de l'usage du véhicule ci-décrit, conformément aux limites minimales exigées par les lois d'assurance en vigueur dans chacune des provinces canadiennes.
AVERTISSEMENT - Quiconque émet ou présente un tel certiﬁcat comme preuve d'une police d'assurance responsabilité qui effectivement n'est pas en vigueur, est coupable d'une infraction passible d'une forte amende et/ou d'emprisonnement et suspension de son permis.
Tout sinistre devrait être rapporté immédiatement.

CANADA INTER-PROVINCE MOTOR VEHICLE LIABILITY INSURANCE CARD
This certiﬁcate is subject to the terms and conditions of the Insurers standard automobile policy.
This certiﬁes that the party named herein is insured against liability for bodily injury and property damage by reason of the operation of the motor vehicle described herein, for an amount not less than the statutory minimum requirements of every province of Canada.
WARNING - Any person who issues or presents such a certiﬁcate as proof of insurance when no policy exists is guilty of fraud and liable to a heavy ﬁ ne and/or imprisonment. His driving permit may also be suspended.
All loss should be reported immediately.

<###NEW_PAGE###>
CLIENT ADVANTAGES
Follow us

Add up the savings by bundling your insurance with us! Visit assureur.ca for more information.
Follow us on Facebook to stay on top of our contests and promotions!
(Facebook.com/Assureur).
Visit our Zone assureur.ca blog  for a wealth of useful information on the insurance industry and financial services.
Your Saving

Discount for choosing Pre-Authorized Payment as your premium payment method
Summary of Client Advantages

Your auto insurance
	Privileged rates for claim-free drivers.
	Automatic coverage if you're driving a leased or borrowed vehicle, in Canada or the United States (the eligibility limit is specified in the Q.E.F. endorsement N 27 of your automobile insurance policy).	Rental costs for a replacement vehicle in the event of a covered loss: Q.E.F. endorsement N 20a of your insurance policy covers the reimbursement of automobile rental costs and, during a trip, additional living expenses incurred, following an insured loss that deprives you of your vehicle while repairs are being made.
The available amounts are indicated in your insurance policy.
	$0 deductible in the event of a hit-and-run, a total loss or for a windshield repair.
	Get an additional 10% discount when you insure another vehicle (car, motorcycle, snowmobile, ATV, boat, caravan or motor home) and your residence with us.
	Get an additional 5% discount by insuring your home with us.
Your legal assistance

	Free legal assistance over the telephone is included with all insurance plans.
	No matter what the problem, our lawyers, all members of the Barreau du Québec, are there for you to inform you of your rights. Confidentiality is guaranteed.
	For a low fee, protect yourself with comprehensive legal insurance by adding Legal Access Insurance to your coverages. Call us to get the details or to take advantage of this offer!
<###NEW_PAGE###>
CLIENT ADVANTAGES
Summary of Client Advantages

Our Claims Department
	Speedy and fair claims process, at your disposal 24/7.
	In the event of a loss involving more than one product that's insured with us, you pay a single deductible, the higher of the two.
	To support your psychological well-being, we are pleased to offer you the free services of professionals to help you overcome the problems experienced following a loss. Totally confidential, this service is offered to all our insureds in the 12 months following a loss covered by an individual insurance policy. For more information, visit Assureur.ca/MentalHealth.

OWNERS FORM
Q.P.F. B 1 – QUEBEC AUTOMOBILE INSURANCE POLICY (2014-03)
Approved by the Autorité des marchés financiers
INTRODUCTION
The following is to provide general explanations concerning the insurance contract to make it easier to understand. These explanations must not be used to create any right or coverage.
In case of any ambiguity or discrepancy between the introduction and the laws applicable to the insurance contract, the terms of the laws will prevail.
1. DOCUMENTS INCLUDED IN INSURANCE CONTRACT
The following documents form part of the insurance contract:
	 This document, i.e., the ''Quebec Automobile Insurance Policy Form (Q.P.F.) B 1 – Owners' Form,'' a standard document approved by the Autorité des marchés financiers.
Note that the ''Declarations'' section of this insurance policy contains information specific to the named insured.
	 The endorsements listed in Item 4, ''Declarations.''
Below is useful information to help understand the insurance contract:
	 Refer to the ''Table of Contents'' to see how the insurance contract is structured and to locate specific information.
	 Words and expressions in bold throughout this document and in the endorsements are explained in the ''Definitions'' section. Note that the endorsements may include their own definitions.
	 The insurance contract should be read as a whole. Consequently, clauses should be interpreted as they relate to each other and considering the entire insurance contract.
	 Coverages described in Section A and Section B are different and apply separately.
	 Words in the singular include the plural.
2. OBLIGATION TO INFORM INSURER
Both before the contract is made and after, any and all information that may influence the risk must be reported to the insurer. Such information must also be reported upon renewal of the insurance contract.
In case of doubt over the obligation to report specific information, it is advisable to contact the insurer.
Among other information, the following must be reported:
	 Any change in the use of the described vehicle.
	 If any persons other than the named insured drive the described vehicle.
	 Any automobile accident or any loss that occurred in the past.
	 Any conviction for an offence under the Highway Safety Code.
	 Any criminal conviction.
	 Any change or addition to the described vehicle.
	 Any change to the personal circumstances of the named insured or drivers.
The obligation to inform the insurer is detailed in Article 5, ''General conditions.''
<###NEW_PAGE###>
OWNERS FORM
Q.P.F. B 1 – QUEBEC AUTOMOBILE INSURANCE POLICY (2014-03)
Approved by the Autorité des marchés financiers

From ________________* to _______________* exclusively.
*at 12:01 A.M. standard time at the address of the named insured.
ITEM 3

ITEM 4
The perils covered by the insurance contract are those for which an amount of insurance, a deductible or an insurance premium is shown in the table below. Coverage is subject to the conditions set out in the insurance contract.
COVERAGE | PERILS | AMOUNT OF INSURANCE AND DEDUCTIBLE | INSURANCE PREMIUM
Section A: Civil liability | Property damage or bodily injury to another person | Amount of insurance: $ |  $
Section B: Damage to insured vehicles | Protection 1 : ''All perils'', Protection 2: Collision and upset, Protection 3: All perils other than collision or upset, Protection 4: Specific perils | Deductible per loss: $ $ $ $ | $ $ $ $

Approved by the Autorité des marchés financiers
