<###NEW_PAGE###>
ENDORSEMENT(S)
FORMING A PART OF YOUR AUTOMOBILE INSURANCE POLICY
2. Original electronic equipment
The expression ''original electronic equipment'' as used in this endorsement means the electronic equipment:
	 that is installed by the manufacturer or the dealer; and
	 that was included in the original purchase price of the specified vehicle.
All other conditions of the insurance contract remain the same.
<###NEW_PAGE###>

We structure this exercise in three progressively stricter stages:

  1. Stage 1 — Free-form extraction
    Prompt GPT-4 without a schema and observe the variability.
  2. Stage 2 — Basic flat schema
    Supply a minimal Pydantic model to stabilise output.
  3. Stage 3 — Advanced nested schema
    Use a comprehensive Pydantic hierarchy to capture full policy complexity.

Stage 1 — Free-form extraction¶

In this first experiment, we ask GPT-4 to pull the driver’s last and first names, vehicle details, premium amount, and contract period without any formal schema. Run the cell multiple times and notice:

  • Thousands separators may appear or vanish.
  • Currency symbols can be included or omitted.
  • Sometimes the output is formatted as bullet points and other times as free-form text.

Although each response is human-readable, the format is too inconsistent for reliable downstream parsing.

Note that in all approaches, the extraction prompt goes into the instructions parameter, while the policy text to be analysed is passed via the input parameter.

In [7]:
instructions_car_insurance_naive = """
You will be given a free-form description of a car insurance contract.
Your job is to extract the driver's last name, the driver's first name, the vehicle, the premium, and the contract period.
"""

contract_information_naive = client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-4.1-nano",
    input=text_content,
    instructions=instructions_car_insurance_naive
)

print(contract_information_naive.output_text)

Last Name: Craig  
First Name: Ryan  
Vehicle: Acura RDX 2010  
Premium: 1047$ (excluding tax)  
Contract Period: From 2022-12-25 to 2023-12-25

Stage 2 — Basic flat schema¶

Next, we define a simple Pydantic model CarInsuranceContract_basic listing only the required fields—each with an explicit type (string or integer). By setting text_format=CarInsuranceContract_basic, the underlying LLM is instructed to return a strictly valid JSON object that conforms to this schema. The result is now fully deterministic and ready for parsing, with (usually) identical output across repeated runs and no ad-hoc cleanup needed.

In [8]:
# 1. Craft class for Structured Output
class CarInsuranceContract_basic(BaseModel):
    driver_last_name: str
    driver_first_name: str
    vehicle_make_and_model: str
    premium: int
    contract_period: str

# 2. Craft a precise instructions prompt for your schema
instructions_car_insurance_basic = """
You will be given a free-form description of a car insurance contract.
Your job is to extract **only** the following fields and return a **strictly valid JSON object** that matches the `CarInsuranceContract_basic` Pydantic schema, without extra commentary or keys.

Schema reference:
- driver_last_name: string
- driver_first_name: string
- vehicle_make_and_model: string
- premium: integer  
- contract_period: string
"""

# 3. Call the LLM with text_format=CarInsuranceContract_basic
contract_information_basic = client.responses.parse(
    model="gpt-4.1-nano",
    input=text_content,
    instructions=instructions_car_insurance_basic,
    text_format=CarInsuranceContract_basic
)

# 4. Display the resulting (JSON) output
print(json.dumps(contract_information_basic.output_parsed.model_dump(), indent=2))

{
  "driver_last_name": "Craig",
  "driver_first_name": "Ryan",
  "vehicle_make_and_model": "Acura RDX 2010",
  "premium": 1047,
  "contract_period": "2022-12-25 to 2023-12-25"
}

Stage 3 — Advanced nested schema¶

Finally, we define a richer Pydantic hierarchy CarInsuranceContract_advanced that mirrors real-world policy complexity through multiple nesting levels and stricter typing—incorporating optional fields, list-typed fields, and enum-constrained fields. This schema includes:

  • Driver – nested model with last_name, first_name, an optional second_first_name, and a birth_date sub-model.
  • Vehicle – nested model with distinct make_and_model and serial_number fields.
  • Miscellaneous – nested model for premium, tax, currency, and a damage_to_insured_vehicles list.

By setting text_format=CarInsuranceContract_advanced, we instruct the LLM to emit a JSON object that matches the specified schema.

In [9]:
# 1. Define class for Structured Output

Month = Literal[
    "January", "February", "March", "April", "May", "June",
    "July", "August", "September", "October", "November", "December"
]  # Explicitly give the possible values for Month via `Literal`

class BirthDate(BaseModel):
    year: int
    month: Month
    day: int

class Driver(BaseModel):
    last_name: str
    first_name: str
    second_first_name: Optional[str] = None  # `Optional` or non-mandatory fields
    birth_date: BirthDate

class Vehicle(BaseModel):
    make_and_model: str
    serial_number: str

class Miscellaneous(BaseModel):
    premium: int
    tax: float
    currency: str
    damage_to_insured_vehicles: List[str]

class CarInsuranceContract_advanced(BaseModel):
    driver: Driver
    vehicle: Vehicle
    miscellaneous: Miscellaneous
    
# 2. Craft a precise instructions prompt for your schema
instructions_car_insurance_advanced = """
You will be given a free-form description of a car insurance contract.
Your job is to extract **only** the following fields and return a **strictly valid JSON object** that matches the `CarInsuranceContract_advanced` Pydantic schema, without extra commentary or keys.

Schema reference:
- driver:
    • last_name: string  
    • first_name: string  
    • second_first_name: string or null  
    • birth_date:
        - year: integer  
        - month: one of ["January","February","March","April","May","June","July","August","September","October","November","December"]  
        - day: integer  
- vehicle:
    • make_and_model: string (e.g. "Acura RDX 2010")  
    • serial_number: string  
- miscellaneous:
    • premium: integer  
    • tax: float  
    • currency: string (e.g. "USD", "EUR")  
    • damage_to_insured_vehicles: list of strings  
      Extract the list of protections **exactly as stated** under the "Section B - DAMAGE TO INSURED VEHICLES" heading in the input text.
      Each entry must match the full phrasing of a listed protection under that section.
"""

# 3. Call your wrapper with text_format=CarInsuranceContract_advanced
contract_information_advanced = client.responses.parse(
    model="gpt-4.1-nano",
    input=text_content,
    instructions=instructions_car_insurance_advanced,
    text_format=CarInsuranceContract_advanced
)

# 4. Inspect the resulting (JSON) output
print(json.dumps(contract_information_advanced.output_parsed.model_dump(), indent=2))

{
  "driver": {
    "last_name": "Craig",
    "first_name": "Ryan",
    "second_first_name": null,
    "birth_date": {
      "year": 1984,
      "month": "July",
      "day": 27
    }
  },
  "vehicle": {
    "make_and_model": "Acura RDX 2010",
    "serial_number": "UE4SMUNYHL50SETQ0"
  },
  "miscellaneous": {
    "premium": 1047,
    "tax": 94.23,
    "currency": "USD",
    "damage_to_insured_vehicles": [
      "All risks Included",
      "Risk of collision and rollover",
      "All risks except collision or rollover",
      "Specific risks Included"
    ]
  }
}

3.3 Further Applications and Resources

Additional Actuarial Applications for Structured Outputs¶

  • Multiclass classification – Structured Outputs enforce predefined classes when categorising document types, car damage types from images, or claim severity levels, ensuring consistent and error-free data for downstream processes.
  • Fraud detection and pattern recognition – Transforming narrative data from customer interactions, adjuster observations, and historical claim patterns into structured datasets to efficiently feed machine learning models for detecting anomalies and fraud risks.
  • Claims settlement automation – Extracting structured information from claim adjuster notes and communications to automate payments, reserving, and auditing workflows, significantly reducing manual interventions and errors.

Further Reading and Resources on Structured Outputs¶

  1. Introducing Structured Outputs in the API

    • Type: Official Blog Announcement
    • Summary: Announces the response_format parameter (Aug 2024) that guarantees model responses exactly match a developer‑supplied JSON Schema, eliminating brittle post‑processing and making downstream actuarial dashboards or databases far more reliable.

  2. Structured Outputs – OpenAI Docs

    • Type: Official Documentation
    • Summary: Provides schema requirements, supported models (gpt‑4o, gpt‑4o‑mini, etc.), and Python snippets showing how to call text_format—a drop‑in strategy to guarantee parseable JSON for batch pricing or claims workloads.

  3. Introduction to Structured Outputs – OpenAI Cookbook

    • Type: Cookbook Notebook
    • Summary: Walk‑through notebook (Aug 2024) demonstrates enforcing a Pydantic schema, measuring failure rates, and highlights common pitfalls such as enum handling—ready to paste into a Python pipeline.

  4. Getting Started With OpenAI Structured Outputs – DataCamp

    • Type: Tutorial
    • Summary: Quick guide (Sep 2024) introducing Pydantic schemas and structured JSON responses via the OpenAI API.

  5. How ChatGPT’s New Structured Output Feature Can Improve Your Investment Game? – Medium

    • Type: Medium Article
    • Summary: Demonstrates defining structured trade models to consistently obtain buy/sell/hold recommendations from ChatGPT.

  6. How to Use Structured Outputs with Azure OpenAI Service

    • Type: Vendor Guide
    • Summary: Shows end‑to‑end Azure example (Apr 2025) defining a Pydantic model, using Structured Outputs, and validating outputs—useful for enterprises already hosting actuarial workloads in Azure.

4. Function Calling

4.1 Concept and Purpose

LLMs excel at understanding and generating natural-language text, but they cannot execute deterministic business logic—such as database look-ups, actuarial calculations, or API calls—on their own. Function Calling (sometimes called Tool Calling) solves this by allowing you to expose a controlled set of back-end functions to the model. The LLM can then decide, at generation time, whether to:

  • answer the user directly, or
  • request that the application run one of the provided functions and feed the result back into the conversation.

This pattern combines the creativity and intent-recognition skills of the LLM with the reliability, speed, and auditability of classical code.

Note

A limitation of Function Calling is that the LLM can only invoke functions that have been explicitly defined and exposed in advance. In contrast, the emerging paradigm of Agentic AI extends this approach: by embedding a Python interpreter (or similar runtime), the system can both generate and execute new code on the fly—dynamically creating functions without the need for prior definition.

Why It Matters for Actuaries¶

  • Repeatability & Compliance – deterministic functions ensure that premium calculations, reserving routines, or regulatory checks are performed exactly the same way every time.
  • Traceability – every function invocation, its arguments, and its result are logged, producing an audit trail that satisfies governance requirements.
  • Scalability – once wrapped as callable tools, anything from a simple NPV formula to a high-performance risk engine can be orchestrated by the model.
  • Lower prompting effort – instead of forcing the model to produce long, perfectly formatted answers, you let code do the heavy lifting and let the LLM focus on deciding what to call and explaining why.

How Function Calling Works¶

The interaction – illustrated in the diagram below – can be described by the following six steps:

  1. Application → LLM
    Send the user prompt together with JSON definitions of the available functions.
  2. LLM reasoning
    Decide whether to respond directly or to invoke one or more tools (e.g., functions); if tools are needed, generate the arguments.
  3. LLM → Application
    Return either a normal assistant message or a special message that contains the chosen function_name and arguments.
  4. Application layer
    Parse the message and execute the requested function(s) with the supplied arguments.
  5. Application → LLM
    Call the LLM again, passing both the original prompt (to preserve context) and the result of the function call.
  6. LLM reasoning and (possibly) final response
    Use the new information to formulate a final answer or trigger additional tool calls, repeating steps 2–5 as needed.

Schematic illustration of the individual stages of Function Calling.
[Source: https://ai.plainenglish.io/a-brief-introduction-to-retrieval-augmented-generation-rag-b7eb70982891]

Since the LLM does not execute code directly, every calculation or data access goes through a sandbox that you control. You decide which functions are exposed and which arguments are valid – an important safety feature when handling sensitive policy or claims data.

4.2 Use Case: Mortality Data Retrieval from Database via Function Calling

In this use case, we show how Function Calling lets an LLM delegate deterministic data retrieval – here, fetching mortality probabilities for men aged 85–100 from an SQLite database – to a Python helper function, then focus on analysis and explanation. This ensures that data access is exact and auditable, while the model handles orchestration and narrative.

Step 1: Application → LLM
First, define our fetch_probabilities function, describe the function as well as its input parameters, register the function in the tools for the LLM, build the user prompt, and send both to the model.

In [10]:
# Define the function to fetch mortality probabilities
def fetch_probabilities(
    mortality_table: str,
    start_age: int,
    end_age: int,
    sex: str = "FEMALE"
):
    """
    Query the 'mortality_tables.sqlite' DB for all records in [start_age..end_age]
    matching the given table name and sex (default FEMALE). Returns, for example:
      [{ "age": 85, "probability": 0.123 }, ...]
    """
    # Normalize inputs
    sex = sex.upper()
    table = mortality_table.replace("'", "''")  # basic escape

    # Build SQL query
    sql = f"""
        SELECT age, probability
          FROM mortality_table
         WHERE mortality_table = '{table}'
           AND gender = '{sex}'
           AND age BETWEEN {start_age} AND {end_age}
         ORDER BY age
    """

    # Run SQL query
    conn = sqlite3.connect("mortality_tables.sqlite")
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute(sql)
    rows = cur.fetchall()
    conn.close()

    return [{"age": age, "probability": prob} for age, prob in rows]


# Register the function `fetch_probabilities` as a callable tool incl. descriptions
tools = [
    {
        "type": "function",
        "name": "fetch_probabilities",
        "description": "Fetch mortality probabilities from an SQLite database",
        "parameters": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "mortality_table": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "description": "Name of the mortality table (e.g. 'PKV-Sterbetafel 2025')"
                },
                "start_age": {
                    "type": "integer",
                    "description": "Start age (inclusive)"
                },
                "end_age": {
                    "type": "integer",
                    "description": "End age (inclusive)"
                },
                "sex": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "enum": ["MALE", "FEMALE"],
                    "description": "Gender to filter by (defaults to FEMALE)",
                    "default": "FEMALE"
                }
            },
            "required": ["mortality_table", "start_age", "end_age"]
        }
    }
]

# Compose user request
user_prompt = (
    'Please fetch the probabilities for men between age 85 and one hundred from the mortality table '
    '"PKV-Sterbetafel 2025". First, present the data in a markdown-formatted table with columns '
    '"Age" and "Probability". Then, analyze the progression of these probabilities: indicate whether '
    'they increase monotonically, highlight any deviations, and summarize the overall trend.'
)
input_messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}]

# Send prompt + function definition to the model
response = client.responses.create(
    model=MODEL_DEFAULT,
    input=input_messages,
    tools=tools
)

Step 2: LLM reasoning
The model decides internally whether to call fetch_probabilities or reply directly. We can check its decision type.

In [11]:
# 2) Inspect the first chunk of the response
first_chunk = response.output[0]
if hasattr(first_chunk, "name"):
    print(f"Model decided to call function: {first_chunk.name}")
else:
    print("Model returned a direct message.")

Model decided to call function: fetch_probabilities

Step 3: LLM → Application
The model returns a function_call message with the function name and JSON arguments.

In [12]:
# Extract the function call details
tool_call = response.output[0]         # contains name, call_id, arguments
args = json.loads(tool_call.arguments) # parse the JSON arguments
print("Invoking:", tool_call.name, "with", args)

Invoking: fetch_probabilities with {'mortality_table': 'PKV-Sterbetafel 2025', 'start_age': 85, 'end_age': 100, 'sex': 'MALE'}

Step 4: Application Layer
Execute the helper function with the generated arguments to fetch the data.

In [13]:
# Run the Python function to get mortality probabilities
fn_result = fetch_probabilities(**args)

Step 5: Application → LLM
Append the function call and its output to the conversation, then re-invoke the model so it can integrate the results.

In [14]:
# Provide the function output back to the LLM for context
input_messages.append(tool_call)
input_messages.append({
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": tool_call.call_id,
    "output": json.dumps(fn_result)
})

# Second API call to generate the final write-up
response_2 = client.responses.create(
    model=MODEL_DEFAULT,
    input=input_messages,
    tools=tools
)

Step 6: LLM reasoning and (possibly) final response
Finally, the LLM uses the fetched probabilities to produce a markdown table and analysis.

In [15]:
# Print GPT's markdown-formatted table + trend analysis
print(response_2.output_text)

Here is the data presented in a markdown-formatted table:

| Age | Probability |
|-----|-------------|
| 85  | 0.06308     |
| 86  | 0.07318     |
| 87  | 0.084803    |
| 88  | 0.098028    |
| 89  | 0.112913    |
| 90  | 0.129465    |
| 91  | 0.147647    |
| 92  | 0.167363    |
| 93  | 0.188107    |
| 94  | 0.209873    |
| 95  | 0.232032    |
| 96  | 0.24325     |
| 97  | 0.25129     |
| 98  | 0.25896     |
| 99  | 0.26621     |
| 100 | 0.27302     |

Analysis of the progression of probabilities:

- The probabilities increase overall with age from 85 to 100.
- The increase is monotonic; there are no decreases or deviations where the probability goes down.
- The values start at 0.06308 at age 85 and steadily rise to 0.27302 by age 100.
- This indicates a consistent increase in mortality probability with advancing age in this range.

Summary:
The mortality probabilities for men aged 85 to 100 in the "PKV-Sterbetafel 2025" table increase monotonically without any deviations, showing a clear trend of increasing mortality risk with higher age.

4.3 Further Applications and Resources

Additional Actuarial Applications for Function Calling¶

  • Dynamic Tariff Calculation – Business users adjust inputs such as coverage limits, deductibles, and risk loadings in chat; the LLM invokes a tariff-calculation function to instantly recalculate quotes, making it easy for non-technical staff to explore “what-if” scenarios.
  • Customer Service with Live Data – Policyholders ask about their renewal dates, payment history, or coverage details; the model triggers API calls to retrieve up-to-date information and presents it in a clear, conversational format.
  • Risk Scenario Simulation – Risk managers describe stress-test parameters in natural language; the LLM calls a simulation engine function to run Monte Carlo scenarios and returns summarized KPIs and distribution charts.
  • Automated Policy Issuance – A non-technical underwriter describes client details in plain language; the model calls a policy-generation function that computes premiums, applies endorsements, and returns a ready-to-issue document without any manual coding.

Further Reading and Resources on Function Calling¶

  1. Function Calling – OpenAI API Guide

    • Type: Official Documentation
    • Summary: Explains the tools and tool_choice parameters, JSON schema definition for arguments, parallel calls, and best‑practice safeguards—foundational for letting an actuarial chatbot trigger premium calculations or database look‑ups.

  2. How to Call Functions with Chat Models – OpenAI Cookbook

    • Type: Cookbook Notebook
    • Summary: Step‑by‑step Python notebook creating a weather‑bot, illustrating clarification questions, forced vs. automatic calls, and execution loop—easy template for actuaries to call Python pricing libraries from GPT‑4o.

  3. OpenAI Function Calling Tutorial: Generate Structured Output

    • Type: Tutorial / Blog Post
    • Summary: Hands‑on guide (Dec 2024) that contrasts “plain prompts” vs. function‑calling, then builds a Python script returning nested JSON—ideal for extracting policy attributes or claim details consistently.

  4. Fine-Tuning for Function Calling – OpenAI Cookbook

    • Type: Cookbook Notebook
    • Summary: Demonstrates how to fine-tune a chat model to improve function-calling accuracy and reliability. Covers assessing baseline performance, generating synthetic prompt-call pairs with GPT-4o, and running fine-tuning jobs—key for complex actuarial toolsets where multiple functions and parameters must be disambiguated.

  5. How to Use Function Calling with Azure OpenAI Service

    • Type: Vendor Guide
    • Summary: Azure‑specific tutorial (Apr 2025) shows single‑ and parallel‑function scenarios, highlights the move from functions→tools, and includes full Python code—ready to integrate with actuarial data stores in Azure.

5. Fine‑Tuning

5.1 Concept and Purpose

Fine-tuning adapts a (pre-trained) LLM to your specific domain by continuing training on a curated set of labelled examples. Unlike prompt engineering – where you provide context and examples at inference time – fine-tuning adjusts the model's internal weights, yielding permanent improvements that no longer require repeated context. In this section, we focus on the standard supervised fine-tuning workflow; alternative approaches such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and reinforcement-based methods also exist but are beyond our scope.

By fine-tuning, you effectively teach the model your "language" – whether that's your company's tone, structure and content of reports, complex insurance rules, or the precise interpretation of structured claim data. The result is a model that delivers more relevant, reliable, and efficient outputs in your actuarial context.

Why It Matters to Actuaries¶

  • Regulatory precision – Fine-tuned models can reproduce mandatory wording (e.g., Solvency II or IFRS 17 disclosures) verbatim, minimizing compliance risks and hallucinations.
  • Data privacy and efficiency – Proprietary inputs such as loss triangles or tariff tables are used only during training; inference no longer requires repeated exposure of sensitive data.
  • Consistent decision-making – Underwriting assessments or claims triage follow the same trained logic every time, reducing reviewer variability.
  • Lower cost and latency – Tailored, fine-tuned models often match the accuracy of much larger general-purpose models, resulting in faster responses and reduced compute costs – critical for production environments.

Note

Model distillation is a form of fine-tuning in which a compact "student" model is trained on the soft-label outputs of a larger "teacher" model. By learning to reproduce the teacher's probability distributions, the student retains most of the teacher's domain knowledge while operating with significantly lower latency and resource demands.

A Three-Step Fine-Tuning Workflow¶

Step 1: Data Preparation
Collect representative prompt–response pairs that reflect your actuarial use case, and format them as required by your LLM provider (e.g., JSONL for OpenAI). Ensure balanced coverage across all relevant categories, then split the dataset into training and validation subsets.
Tip: OpenAI recommends at least 50 high-quality examples per task (the absolute minimum is 10); see the OpenAI Fine-Tuning guide for details.

Step 2: Training
Upload your dataset to your chosen platform (e.g., OpenAI's fine-tuning endpoint) and launch a fine-tuning job. Optionally, configure hyperparameters – such as learning rate, batch size, and number of epochs – to suit your training objectives.

Step 3: Deployment & Governance
After fine-tuning completes, the new model obtaines a unique identifier. Use this ID in your API calls to invoke the fine-tuned model.

The duration of a fine-tuning job varies with the volume of training data – it may finish in minutes or take several hours. Upon successful completion, you'll receive an email alert at your registered address. Below is a sample notification message.


Notification from OpenAI confirming that the fine-tuning job completed successfully and displaying the fine-tuned model’s identifier.
[Source: Email from OpenAI.]

5.2 Use Case: Improving Car Damage Classification by Fine-Tuning Vision-Enabled LLMs

In this case study, we use a dataset on car damages sourced from a Kaggle competition hosted by Analytics Vidhya, a platform dedicated to data science education and challenges. The dataset contains thousands of car images, each labeled with one of six damage categories: crack, scratch, tire flat, dent, glass shatter, or lamp broken. Our objective is to demonstrate that a fine-tuned LLM-based classifier delivers higher accuracy in damage categorization than its non-fine-tuned counterpart.

The following code snippet uses the kagglehub library to download the latest version of the dataset directly from Kaggle. Note that for the respective code to work properly, you must have a Kaggle account and configure your Kaggle environment (for example, by placing your Kaggle API credentials in the appropriate location) to access and import the dataset directly. Alternatively, you can also download the dataset from the source above.

In [16]:
# Download latest version from Kaggle
path = kagglehub.dataset_download("imnandini/analytics-vidya-ripik-ai-hackfest")

Warning: Looks like you're using an outdated `kagglehub` version (installed: 0.3.12), please consider upgrading to the latest version (0.3.13).

In the code below, we load the dataset and define a label map to translate numeric class IDs into human-readable damage categories. We then display the first eight records to give a quick overview of the dataset's structure and contents.

In [17]:
# Create a mapping for the labels
label_mapping = {
    1: "crack",
    2: "scratch",
    3: "tire flat",
    4: "dent",
    5: "glass shatter",
    6: "lamp broken"
}

# Load dataset
df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(path, "train/train/train.csv"))

# Rename label column to label_id for clarity and adjust to start from 0
df.rename(columns={'label': 'label_id'}, inplace=True)

# Add label_text column using the mapped values
df['label_text'] = df['label_id'].map(label_mapping)

# Display the first 8 rows of the updated dataframe
df.head(8)
Out[17]:
image_id filename label_id label_text
0 1 1.jpg 2 scratch
1 2 2.jpg 4 dent
2 3 3.jpg 2 scratch
3 4 4.jpg 3 tire flat
4 5 5.jpg 5 glass shatter
5 6 6.jpg 2 scratch
6 7 7.jpg 5 glass shatter
7 8 8.jpg 5 glass shatter

The dataset consists not only of the tabular metadata shown above but also of the corresponding JPEG images. The following code combines the first eight images with their associated metadata to provide a visual impression of the damage types alongside their descriptive information.

In [18]:
# Define the folder path for the images corresponding to the filenames of df
image_folder = os.path.join(path, "train/train/images/")

# Prepare to display the first 8 images in a 2x5 grid
num_images = 8
num_cols = 4
num_rows = 2
fig, axs = plt.subplots(num_rows, num_cols, figsize=(15, 8))

# Iterate through the first 8 images
for idx in range(num_images):
    # Calculate the row and column indices for the current subplot
    row = idx // num_cols
    col = idx % num_cols

    # Get metadata for the current image
    filename = df.loc[idx, 'filename']
    label_id = df.loc[idx, 'label_id']
    label_text = df.loc[idx, 'label_text']

    # Open image and display it
    with Image.open(os.path.join(image_folder, filename)) as img:
        axs[row, col].imshow(img)
        axs[row, col].axis('off')

    # Add all metadata as left-aligned text below the image
    axs[row, col].text(
        0, -0.05,
        f"filename:     {filename}\n"
        f"label_id:     {label_id}\n"
        f"label_text:   {label_text}",
        fontsize=12,
        va='top',
        ha='left',
        transform=axs[row, col].transAxes,  # Use axis-relative coordinates
        wrap=True,
        fontfamily='monospace'
    )

# Adjust layout to prevent overlap
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

In order to speed up the model training process and reduce the costs of fine-tuning the LLM, we start by creating a smaller subset of the dataset (1500 samples). This smaller sample is drawn randomly to ensure diversity while keeping the dataset manageable. Following that, we split the data into training (60%, 900 entries), validation (20%, 300 entries), and test (20%, entries) sets, ensuring that the distribution of damage types is consistent across all sets using stratified sampling.

In [19]:
# Sample 1500 entries from the original dataframe
df_sampled = df.sample(1500, random_state=123).reset_index(drop=True)

# Split the data into train (60%), validation (20%), and test (20%) sets
df_train, df_test = train_test_split(df_sampled, test_size=0.2, stratify=df_sampled['label_id'], random_state=123)
df_train, df_val = train_test_split(df_train, test_size=0.25, stratify=df_train['label_id'], random_state=123)

To systematically store and later evaluate the model predictions alongside the actual labels, we first initialize a data structure that holds the ground-truth labels from the test dataset. Predictions from the non-fine-tuned model and the fine-tuned model will be added in the following subsections.

In [20]:
# Initialize a results DataFrame with actual labels from the test set
results_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "filename": df_test["filename"],
    "actual_label_id": df_test["label_id"]
    # Predictions from different models will be added later
})

Non-Fine-Tuned LLM¶

First, we explore the use of a non-fine-tuned Large Language Model for the damage classification task. Specifically, we employ an off-the-shelf version of GPT-4o, a multimodal model inherently equipped with vision capabilities, to predict the type of car damage directly from the image data.

To enable GPT-4o to process the images, we first encode them into a Base64 string representation, effectively converting the binary image data into a text format suitable for model input.

In [21]:
# Function to encode the image
def encode_image(image_path):
    with open(image_path, "rb") as image_file:
        return base64.b64encode(image_file.read()).decode("utf-8")

Before making predictions with GPT-4o, we define a system prompt for the instructions parameter that will be provided during each model call. This prompt clearly instructs the LLM to classify the damage type visible in the image into exactly one of the six predefined categories. It emphasizes strict adherence to the specified output format to ensure consistency and accuracy across all predictions.

In [22]:
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
You will classify images of cars with visible damages into one of six specific classes. Your task is to examine the image provided, identify the type of damage, and return the correct damage type as one of the predefined classes. The six damage classes are as follows:

1. crack
2. scratch
3. tire flat
4. dent
5. glass shatter
6. lamp broken

# Steps

1. **Analyze the Image:**
   - Carefully inspect the image provided, focusing on visible damage to determine its type.
   - Pay attention to details such as patterns, locations, and characteristics of the damage to accurately classify it.

2. **Identify the Correct Class:**
   - Match the observed damage to one of the six predefined classes listed above.
   - Avoid ambiguous classifications and ensure the answer aligns precisely with the provided options.

3. **Output the Classification:**
   - Provide your answer in a concise format with **only the name of the damage class** (e.g., "scratch").
   - Do not add any additional text, reasoning, or explanations. The response must be exactly one of the six class names.

# Notes
- If multiple damage types appear equally prominent in the image, select the one that seems most severe or predominant.
- Ensure every response falls strictly within the six defined classes. Avoid assumptions or interpretations beyond the scope of these categories.

# Example Format
For an input image with a visible scratch:
- Correct Output: "scratch"

For an input image showing a shattered glass window:
- Correct Output: "glass shatter"

Adhere to this strict output format and guidelines in every classification.
"""

Although the LLM is instructed to return exaclty one of the six specified damage types, variations in the output (e.g., different quotation styles such as scratch, 'scratch', or "scratch") may occur. To enforce a consistent and strict output format, we employ, again, the concept of Structured Outputs introduced in Section 3. The code below defines a response schema using a Pydantic model that restricts the prediction to one of the six damage classes.

In [23]:
# Define the response format for the OpenAI model using Structured Outputs
class ResponseFormat(BaseModel):
    prediction: Literal["crack", "scratch", "tire flat", "dent", "glass shatter", "lamp broken"]

In the following function, we query an LLM with vision capabilities by providing the base64-encoded textual representation of an image, along with the previously defined system prompt and Structured Outputs (via the text_format parameter), to predict the corresponding damage type.

In [24]:
# Helper function to get prediction from an OpenAI model
def get_llm_image_prediction(encoded_image, model_name, system_prompt):
    """
    Queries an LLM with vision capabilities to predict a label from an encoded image.
    """
    try:
        response = client.beta.chat.completions.parse(
            model=model_name,
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": [{"type": "text", "text": system_prompt}]},
                {"role": "user", "content": [{"type": "image_url", 
                                              "image_url": {"url": f"data:image/jpeg;base64,{encoded_image}", 
                                                            "detail": "low"}}]}
            ],
            response_format=ResponseFormat
        )
        return response.choices[0].message.parsed.prediction
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error getting prediction from model '{model_name}': {e}")
        return None

Below, we obtain predictions for each test image using the non-fine-tuned, off-the-shelf GPT-4o. Each image is encoded in Base64, the LLM's textual output is then mapped back to its corresponding label ID via a reverse mapping, and the resulting predictions are stored in our results DataFrame for later evaluation.

In [25]:
# Set model name of the non-fine-tuned LLM
non_fine_tuned_llm_model = "gpt-4o-2024-08-06"

# Create a reverse mapping from label text to label ID
label_text_to_id = {v: k for k, v in label_mapping.items()}

# Obtain non-fine-tuned LLM predictions for each test image
non_fine_tuned_predictions = []
for _, row in df_test.iterrows():
    image_path = os.path.join(image_folder, row['filename'])
    encoded_image = encode_image(image_path)
    
    # Retrieve the non-fine-tuned LLM's prediction (as label text)
    pred_label_text = get_llm_image_prediction(encoded_image, non_fine_tuned_llm_model, SYSTEM_PROMPT)
    
    # Map the predicted label text back to its corresponding label ID
    pred_label_id = label_text_to_id.get(pred_label_text, None)
    non_fine_tuned_predictions.append(pred_label_id)

# Store the non-fine-tuned LLM predictions in the results DataFrame
results_df["non_fine_tuned_llm_predicted_label_id"] = non_fine_tuned_predictions

Fine-Tuned LLM¶

To fine-tune our vision-enabled GPT-4o model on domain-specific data, we must first prepare the dataset in the JSONL format required for OpenAI's fine-tuning. Each training example is structured as a series of messages—including a system prompt that corresponds to the previously defined instructions and defines the task, a user message containing the base64-encoded image, and (optionally) an assistant message with the ground truth label—to ensure consistent and reliable outputs according to OpenAI's Structured Outputs framework. The following function generates a JSONL file from our dataset:

In [26]:
def create_jsonl(input_df, img_folder_path, output_file, include_assistant=True):
    """
    Generates a JSONL file from the input DataFrame.

    Parameters:
    - input_df: DataFrame containing the input data.
    - img_folder_path: Path to the folder containing the images.
    - output_file: Output JSONL file path.
    - include_assistant: Boolean to determine if the assistant message is included.
    """
    json_data = []

    for idx, row in tqdm(input_df.iterrows(), total=len(input_df)):
        # Encode the image in base64 format
        image_path = os.path.join(img_folder_path, row['filename'])
        encoded_image = encode_image(image_path)
        
        # System message
        system_message = {
            "role": "system",
            "content": [{"type": "text", "text": SYSTEM_PROMPT}]
        }
        
        # User message (image input)
        user_message = {
            "role": "user",
            "content": [
                {"type": "image_url", "image_url": {"url": f"data:image/jpeg;base64,{encoded_image}", "detail": "low"}}
            ]
        }
        
        # Combine system and user messages
        all_messages = [system_message, user_message]

        # Add assistant message if specified
        if include_assistant:
            assistant_message = {
                "role": "assistant",
                "content": [{"type": "text", "text": row["label_text"]}]
            }
            all_messages.append(assistant_message)
        
        # Add to JSON data
        json_data.append({"messages": all_messages})

    # Save to JSONL file
    with open(output_file, "w") as file:
        for entry in json_data:
            file.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")

Next, we generate JSONL files for each data split—training, validation, and test. The training and validation files include the assistant messages (i.e., the correct labels), whereas the test file is generated without them, as it will be used solely for evaluation during fine-tuning.

In [27]:
# Create JSONL for the training dataset
create_jsonl(
    input_df=df_train,
    img_folder_path=image_folder,
    output_file="visual_fine_tuning_train.jsonl",
    include_assistant=True
)

# Create JSONL for the validation dataset
create_jsonl(
    input_df=df_val,
    img_folder_path=image_folder,
    output_file="visual_fine_tuning_validation.jsonl",
    include_assistant=True
)

# Create JSONL for the test dataset without assistant messages
create_jsonl(
    input_df=df_test,
    img_folder_path=image_folder,
    output_file="visual_fine_tuning_test.jsonl",
    include_assistant=False
)

100%|██████████| 900/900 [00:01<00:00, 513.15it/s]
100%|██████████| 300/300 [00:00<00:00, 492.59it/s]
100%|██████████| 300/300 [00:00<00:00, 1604.71it/s]

Finally, if we choose to proceed with fine-tuning, we upload the training and validation JSONL files to the OpenAI platform and initiate a fine-tuning job using the vision-enabled GPT-4o model. The code snippet below demonstrates how to upload these files and create the fine-tuning job, controlled by the flag create_fine_tuning_job.

In [28]:
create_fine_tuning_job = False
if create_fine_tuning_job:
    # upload training file
    train_file = client.files.create(
        file=open("visual_fine_tuning_train.jsonl", "rb"),
        purpose="fine-tune"
    )

    # upload validation file
    val_file = client.files.create(
        file=open("visual_fine_tuning_validation.jsonl", "rb"),
        purpose="fine-tune"
    )

    # create fine tuning job
    client.fine_tuning.jobs.create(
        training_file=train_file.id,
        validation_file=val_file.id,
        model="gpt-4o-2024-08-06"
    )

To conclude, we obtain predictions for each test image using our fine-tuned vision-enabled LLM. In this step, each image is encoded to Base64 and fed to the model via the same prediction function used for the non-fine-tuned LLM, after which the output text is mapped back to its corresponding label ID and stored in the results DataFrame.

In [29]:
# Set model name of the fine-tuned LLM
fine_tuned_llm_model = "ft:gpt-4o-2024-08-06:personal::B5swMoBf"

# Obtain fine-tuned LLM predictions for each test image
fine_tuned_predictions = []
for _, row in df_test.iterrows():
    image_path = os.path.join(image_folder, row['filename'])
    encoded_image = encode_image(image_path)
    
    # Retrieve the fine-tuned LLM's prediction (as label text)
    pred_label_text = get_llm_image_prediction(encoded_image, fine_tuned_llm_model, SYSTEM_PROMPT)
    
    # Map the predicted label text back to its corresponding label ID
    pred_label_id = label_text_to_id.get(pred_label_text, None)
    fine_tuned_predictions.append(pred_label_id)

# Store the fine-tuned LLM predictions in the results DataFrame
results_df["fine_tuned_llm_predicted_label_id"] = fine_tuned_predictions

Finally, we quantitatively assess our models by computing accuracy on the test set, which measures the proportion of correctly classified images. Accuracy is only one performance metric; one could also evaluate (weighted) F1 scores, inspect confusion matrices, or review cases where the two models disagree to gain deeper insights.

In [30]:
# Extract labels
actual_labels = results_df["actual_label_id"]
non_fine_tuned_labels = results_df["non_fine_tuned_llm_predicted_label_id"]
fine_tuned_labels = results_df["fine_tuned_llm_predicted_label_id"]

# Compute accuracy and weighted F1-score, rounded to three decimal places
metrics = {
    "Model": ["Non-Fine-Tuned LLM", "Fine-Tuned LLM"],
    "Accuracy": [
        round(accuracy_score(actual_labels, non_fine_tuned_labels), 3),
        round(accuracy_score(actual_labels, fine_tuned_labels), 3)
    ]
}

# Create DataFrame
metrics_df = pd.DataFrame(metrics)
metrics_df
Out[30]:
Model Accuracy
0 Non-Fine-Tuned LLM 0.86
1 Fine-Tuned LLM 0.89

As the table shows, fine-tuning yields a clear improvement in accuracy over the baseline model, confirming the value of domain-specific adaptation.

5.3 Further Applications and Resources

Additional Actuarial Applications for Fine-Tuning¶

  • Consistent and Efficient Report Generation – Fine-tuned models leverage historical documents and associated numerical data—from actuarial reserves and risk assessments to technical documentation and sustainability reports—to reliably produce new reports or specific report sections. Users simply input updated figures, and the model automatically generates consistent, compliant, and professionally styled outputs, significantly streamlining reporting workflows.
  • Customer Service Automation – Chatbots trained on policy documentation and FAQs accurately handle customer inquiries on coverage and premiums, providing consistent responses in the company's style and voice.
  • Fraud Detection & Claims Triage – Models fine-tuned on historical fraud cases rapidly flag suspicious claims or route complex cases to specialists, improving efficiency and accuracy of fraud detection.
  • Automated Claims Assessment – Fine-tuned models quickly categorize claims based on damage descriptions, photos, and adjuster notes, streamlining settlements and significantly reducing processing times.

Further Reading and Resources on Fine-Tuning¶

  1. Fine‑Tuning Now Available for GPT‑4o

    • Type: Official Blog Announcement
    • Summary: Details Aug 2024 launch of GPT‑4o fine‑tuning, token pricing, and early results showing strong gains from a few dozen domain‑specific examples—helpful when you need the model to use actuarial jargon or calculation styles.

  2. How to Fine‑Tune Chat Models – OpenAI Cookbook

    • Type: Cookbook Notebook
    • Summary: Full Python workflow (Jul 2024) covering dataset prep, upload, job creation, and evaluation; includes a cost estimator and token‑count utility—pragmatic template for building a custom reserving‑assistant model.

  3. Fine-Tuned Classification – OpenAI Cookbook

    • Type: Cookbook Notebook
    • Summary: Detailed tutorial (updated Mar 2025) demonstrating fine-tuning a babbage-002 model for classification tasks. It covers data formatting, JSONL dataset uploading, initiating fine-tuning jobs, and post-training evaluation—an excellent resource for actuaries classifying claims as fraudulent or legitimate, or assigning underwriting risk categories.

  4. Fine‑Tuning – OpenAI API Documentation

    • Type: Official Documentation
    • Summary: Authoritative reference on data format, required example count, validation splits, and CLI vs. API job control—key for governance/compliance sign‑off on actuarial fine‑tunes.

  5. Azure OpenAI GPT‑4o‑mini Fine‑Tuning Tutorial

    • Type: Vendor Tutorial
    • Summary: March 2025 walk‑through that fine‑tunes gpt‑4o‑mini‑2024‑07‑18, covers dataset creation, job monitoring, deployment, and cost caveats—perfect for insurers leveraging Azure for model hosting.

  6. Case Study: Car Damage Classification and Localization with Fine-Tuned Vision-Enabled LLMs

    • Type: Case Study
    • Summary: Jupyter Notebook with more detailed information on how to use fine-tuned vision-enabled LLMs for car damage classfication and localization.

6. Retrieval‑Augmented Generation

6.1 Concept and Purpose

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) combines LLMs with external data sources to produce responses that are not only linguistically coherent but also factually grounded. The core principle behind RAG is that instead of relying entirely on knowledge embedded within the model, it selectively accesses external resources—such as databases, document repositories, or APIs—whenever specific and up-to-date information is required.

By integrating the natural-language strengths of LLMs with the precision of external data sources, RAG helps produce outputs that are:

  • Current, as external information can be continuously updated and seamlessly incorporated.
  • Accurate, by directly accessing relevant, authoritative data sources rather than relying on generalized knowledge.
  • Flexible, because new data sources can be quickly added or existing sources easily updated, allowing dynamic adaptation to changing information needs.

The implementation of a typical RAG workflow consists of five main stages, as illustrated in Figure 5:

  1. Indexing (Chunking, Vectorize & Store)

    • Source materials (e.g., actuarial reports, policies, or regulations) are segmented into smaller, manageable pieces ("chunks"). This enables precise, targeted retrieval.
    • Each chunk is transformed into a semantic vector, mathematically representing its meaning in a high-dimensional space. These vectors are stored in a specialized, optimized data structure (such as a vector database) that allows rapid and accurate retrieval.

  2. Vectorize & Search

    • The user query is similarly transformed into a semantic vector, capturing its meaning and intent.
    • A search algorithm compares this query vector against the stored document vectors, identifying the most semantically similar chunks using an appropriate similarity metric, ensuring highly relevant retrieval results.

  3. Retrieve

    • Based on the query-vector comparison, the system fetches the most relevant chunks or documents from the vector database. These retrieved documents contain information directly applicable to the user's query.

  4. Augment

    • The original query, retrieved chunks, and any additional contextual instructions or system prompts are combined into an enriched prompt. This augmented prompt provides the LLM with both the user's question and the necessary external context for accurate answering.

  5. Generate

    • The LLM processes the enriched prompt, synthesizing the retrieved external information with its inherent linguistic and contextual understanding, ultimately producing a coherent, informative, and contextually relevant response.

By following this structured approach, RAG enables actuaries to leverage the interpretative capabilities of LLMs alongside continuously updated domain-specific data, ensuring outputs remain precise, compliant, and fully aligned with current actuarial practice.


Schematic illustration of the individual phases of RAG.
[Source: https://ai.plainenglish.io/a-brief-introduction-to-retrieval-augmented-generation-rag-b7eb70982891]

Note

RAG is an emerging field, yielding new approaches like GraphRAG from Microsoft, which extends RAG by also incorporating a knowledge graph representing semantic relations between relevant entities, or AgenticRAG approaches, where the query generation is improved by an underlying agent.

6.2 Use Case: Extracting KPIs from Annual Reports with RAG

Before we start, we define some constants that will be used in our RAG system.

In [31]:
# Specify the large language model and embeddings model to be used
LARGE_LANGUAGE_MODEL = "gpt-4.1-mini"
EMBEDDINGS_MODEL = "text-embedding-3-small"

# Set parameters for text chunking and retrieval to manage context and similarity search
CHUNK_SIZE = 2000  # Size of text chunks in characters
OVERLAP = 300      # Overlap between text chunks for context coherence
TOP_N = 5          # Number of top similar chunks to retrieve
THRESHOLD = 0.5    # Threshold for cosine similarity to consider chunks as similar

# Link to Generali's Annual Report from 2024
GENERALI_ANNUAL_REPORT_LINK = "https://www.generali.com/doc/jcr:259c5d6e-46f7-4a43-9512-58e5dcbd2a56/Annual%20Integrated%20Report%20and%20Consolidated%20Financial%20Statements%202024_Generali%20Group_final_interactive.pdf/lang:en/Annual_Integrated_Report_and_Consolidated_Financial_Statements_2024_Generali_Group_final_interactive.pdf"

Stage 1: Preprocessing¶

We first define a function to load PDF files from a specified folder and extract their text content. This function scans the folder for PDFs, opens each document, and retrieves the text from all pages, preparing the documents for cleansing, chunking, and vectorization.

In [32]:
def load_pdf_from_url(url: str):
    """
    Fetches a PDF from the given URL into memory and returns its extracted text.

    :param url: Direct URL to the PDF.
    :return: A dict with 'filename' (derived from URL) and 'text' (full document text).
    """
    # 1. Fetch PDF bytes
    resp = requests.get(url)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    pdf_bytes = resp.content

    # 2. Derive a filename from the URL path
    path = urlparse(url).path
    filename = unquote(path.split("/")[-1])

    # 3. Open PDF from bytes and extract text
    doc = fitz.open(stream=pdf_bytes, filetype="pdf")
    full_text = []
    for page in doc:
        full_text.append(page.get_text())
    text = "\n".join(full_text)

    return {"filename": filename, "text": text}

Before proceeding with chunking and vectorization, we define a function to clean the input text. This function removes unnecessary characters, redundant spaces, and empty lines, ensuring that the text is properly formatted for the next steps in the process.

In [33]:
def clean_text(text):
    """
    Cleans a text segment by removing unwanted characters and patterns.

    :param text: Input text segment.
    :return: Cleaned text segment.
    """
    # Remove extra spaces between words
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[ \t]+', ' ', text)
    # Remove leading and trailing spaces
    cleaned_text = cleaned_text.strip()
    # Remove empty lines
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'\n\s*\n', '\n', cleaned_text)
    return cleaned_text

Next, we define a function to split the cleaned text into overlapping chunks. The chunking is done by breaking the text after a fixed number of characters, with an overlap between adjacent chunks. This ensures that each chunk contains enough context for retrieval tasks. An alternative approach could involve splitting by tokens or ensuring that chunks don't cut through sentences, but for simplicity, we use character-based chunking in this example.

In [34]:
def create_chunks(text, chunk_size=CHUNK_SIZE, overlap=OVERLAP):
    """
    Splits text into overlapping chunks.

    :param text: Input text to be split into chunks.
    :param chunk_size: Target size of each chunk.
    :param overlap: Overlap between adjacent chunks.
    :return: List of text chunks.
    """
    if chunk_size <= overlap:
        raise ValueError("chunk_size must be greater than overlap.")
    return [
        text[i: i + chunk_size]
        for i in range(0, len(text), chunk_size - overlap)
    ]

To generate embeddings for the text, we use a pre-trained OpenAI embeddings model. The embeddings represent the semantic meaning of the text as a high-dimensional numerical vector, which can be used for similarity-based retrieval. While we use OpenAI's embeddings model in this example, readers are free to explore other models depending on their specific needs.

In [35]:
def generate_text_embedding(chunk, model=EMBEDDINGS_MODEL):
    """
    Generates a numerical embedding for a given text chunk using a specified model.

    :param chunk: The text chunk for which the embedding is generated.
    :param model: The embedding model to be used (default is EMBEDDINGS_MODEL).
    :return: A list of float values representing the embedding of the input text chunk.
    """
    try:
        response = client.embeddings.create(
            model=model,
            input=chunk,
            encoding_format="float"
        )
        embedding = response.data[0].embedding
        return embedding
    except Exception as e:
        logging.error(f"Error generating embeddings for text: {e}")
        return None

Before generating embeddings, we first clean and chunk the document text using the previously defined functions. The text is cleansed to remove unwanted characters and then split into overlapping chunks. Finally, embeddings are generated for each chunk. This process prepares the text for similarity-based retrieval.

In [36]:
def generate_document_embeddings(document, chunk_size=CHUNK_SIZE, overlap=OVERLAP):
    """
    Processes a list of documents by cleansing their content, creating chunks, 
    and generating embeddings for each chunk.

    :param docs: List of documents containing 'filename' and 'text'.
    :param chunk_size: Target size of each text chunk.
    :param overlap: Overlap between adjacent chunks.
    :return: List of dictionaries containing 'filename', 'content', and 'embedding' for each chunk.
    """
    embeddings_data = []

    # Clean the text
    cleaned_document = clean_text(document)

    # Create chunks
    chunks = create_chunks(cleaned_document, chunk_size, overlap)

    # Generate embeddings for each chunk
    for chunk in chunks:
        embedding = generate_text_embedding(chunk)
        if embedding is not None:
            embeddings_data.append({
                "content": chunk,
                "embedding": embedding
            })
    
    return embeddings_data

We create a dictionary to store the embeddings DataFrames for each insurance company. For each company, we load the corresponding documents, generate embeddings, and store the results in a DataFrame. Note that this step may take several minutes.

In [37]:
# Dictionary to store embeddings DataFrames for each company
company_embeddings_dict = {}

# Load documents
document = load_pdf_from_url(GENERALI_ANNUAL_REPORT_LINK)

# Process documents and generate embeddings
document_embeddings = generate_document_embeddings(document["text"])

# Create a DataFrame for the current company's embeddings
company_name = "Generali"
company_embeddings_dict[company_name] = pd.DataFrame(document_embeddings)

To verify the correct processing of Stage 1, we display one random row from the Generali embeddings DataFrame. This allows us to check if the text chunking and embedding generation have been performed correctly for this company.

In [38]:
# Display one random row for the company "Generali"
company_embeddings_dict.get(company_name).sample(1)
Out[38]:
content embedding
529 it are defined by the Group Legal entities following centrally defined Group rules \nthat vary on the basis of the product’s features and type of coverage:\n• in the case of Saving contracts, the coverage units are generally defined as a function of the assets under management;\n• in the case of contracts providing only insurance services, the coverage units are generally defined as a function of the sum insured;\n• in the case of contracts that envisage a bundling of services, hybrid approaches are generally adopted (e.g., combination of assets \nunder management and sum insured).\n244\nAnnual Integrated Report and Consolidated Financial Statements 2024\nGenerali Group\nFuture coverage units used to determine the CSM release are generally discounted. In detail:\n• for the GMM business, coverage units are discounted using the reference locked-in curve of each group of insurance contracts,\n• for the VFA business, in order to avoid a non-representative CSM release volatility caused by the fluctuations of interest rates, a \n10- year rolling weighted average curve is applied.\nThe coverage unit mechanics spread over the duration of the insurance contracts the opening and new business CSM as well as the \nvariances, including the systematic economic variance, defined as the impact on the CSM of the excess of real-world returns over \nrisk-free rates over a projection horizon that is consistent with the reporting period.\nAccording to the Group’s position, this “systematic” variance reflects the investment-related services provided to the policyholder \nand, as such, consistently with IFRS 17 requirements, is considered as an adjustment to the coverage units of the reporting period. \nThis approach allows the avoidance of the deferral of the systematic economic variance and its concentration towards the end of the \nprojection horizon (so-called “bow-wave” effect).\nThe systematic economic variance is determined with expected hypotheses defined at the beginning of the reporting period assu [0.0073534846, 0.02643166, 0.07489371, 0.03278103, -0.038192425, 0.009632284, -0.012626589, -0.024291152, 0.036268372, 0.04540762, -0.035450652, -0.033310145, -0.022535454, 0.015320261, 0.02349748, 0.022222796, 0.098078534, -0.019444946, 0.04170382, -0.034825336, -0.026864571, 0.03771141, -0.033454448, 0.022439253, -0.05401775, 0.027345585, 0.021477226, 0.018915832, 0.03203546, -0.0013926827, -0.023016468, -0.012590513, -0.042377237, 0.019420896, 0.01617406, -0.03280508, -0.012295892, -0.019805707, 0.056038003, -0.017292414, -0.036003817, -0.02069558, 0.013756969, 0.045551922, 0.00014176728, -0.023689887, 0.055220284, -0.013829121, -0.018987985, -0.00471092, 8.4647e-05, -0.0019135295, -0.018266466, -0.03761521, 0.026407609, 0.009163296, 0.0007989324, 0.006818358, -0.017316464, -0.011057285, 0.02218672, -0.023569632, 0.011496209, -0.053488635, 0.028067103, -0.010768676, -0.028163306, 0.017015832, 0.0023704916, -0.021645581, 0.0071670925, ...]

Stage 2: Prompt Augmenting¶

While the first stage is largely independent of the specific information we aim to extract and needs to be conducted only once, Stage 2 focuses on identifying the relevant context within the input documents for each of the three key aspects (regulatory capital ratios under Solvency II or SST, discount rates for insurance contract valuations by year and currency, and strategies for assessing and mitigating cyber risk).

We start by defining a system prompt that provides context for the AI assistant. This prompt is used for each query to ensure consistent extraction of structured information from the documents.

In [39]:
# Define an enhanced system prompt for consistent and accurate data extraction
system_prompt = '''
You are an AI assistant specialized in extracting and structuring key financial and risk insights from annual reports of European insurance companies. Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation to retrieve precise text segments and apply Structured Outputs to format data consistently. Identify and extract regulatory ratios, discount rates, and cyber risk strategies with exact numerical formats and contextual clarity. Ensure all outputs follow the specified schema for seamless integration into actuarial workflows and to support robust comparative analysis.
'''

To ensure relevant retrieval, we define detailed queries that guide the similarity search. Each query specifies the required data, attributes, and format, ensuring the extracted information is focused on the three key aspects solvency ratios, discount rates, and cybersecurity risk strategies.

In [40]:
# Define queries for retrieving structured financial insights

# Query for extracting regulatory compliance ratios specifically for the year 2023
query_solvency_ratio = '''
Extract the group's solvency capital ratio in percentage for 2024, along with the regulatory framework (Solvency II or SST).
'''

# Query for retrieving discount rate data specifically for the year 2024
query_discount_rates = '''
Extract the discount rates for financial or insurance contract liabilities in 2024, using only currency EUR. For each duration (e.g., 1 year, 5 years, 10 years, 20 years, 40 years, etc.), extract the corresponding discount rate in percentage. Ensure that the data reflects the rates as of December 31, 2024. If no specific approach is mentioned, assume non-VFA, unit-linked contracts, or liquid products.
'''

# Query for identifying cybersecurity risk management strategies
query_cyber_risk_strategies = '''
Extract the insurer's documented approach to cyber-risk assessment and mitigation. Return each policy, process, or control as a separate text bullet, and include any references to governance bodies, frequency of reviews, or related quantitative metrics if available.
'''

# Store all queries in a list for easy access
queries = [query_solvency_ratio, query_discount_rates, query_cyber_risk_strategies]

Once we have our document embeddings and search query, this function performs vector-based similarity search to identify the most relevant text chunks. It generates an embedding for the query, calculates cosine similarity with document chunks, and ranks the results based on their similarity scores. The top matching snippets are then formatted into a structured output, ensuring that only the most relevant content is returned, ready to be used in an augmented prompt for the AI model.

In [41]:
def retrieve_top_matching_chunks(document_embeddings, search_query, max_results=TOP_N, similarity_cutoff=THRESHOLD):
    """
    Identifies and formats the most relevant document chunks based on semantic similarity to a query.

    :param document_embeddings: List of document chunk embeddings.
    :param search_query: The query used to find relevant content.
    :param max_results: Maximum number of top matching results to return.
    :param similarity_cutoff: Minimum similarity score required for inclusion.
    :return: A formatted string containing the most relevant text snippets.
    """
    try:
        # Generate embedding for the query
        query_embedding = generate_text_embedding(search_query)
        if query_embedding is None:
            logging.error("Failed to generate query embedding.")
            return "No relevant content found."

        # Reshape query embedding for similarity calculation
        query_vector = np.array(query_embedding).reshape(1, -1)

        # Compute cosine similarity for each document embedding
        def compute_similarity(doc_vector):
            doc_vector_reshaped = np.array(doc_vector).reshape(1, -1)
            return cosine_similarity(query_vector, doc_vector_reshaped)[0][0]

        # Convert document embeddings into a DataFrame and compute similarity scores
        df_embeddings = pd.DataFrame(document_embeddings)
        df_embeddings['similarity'] = df_embeddings['embedding'].apply(compute_similarity)

        # Filter relevant results based on similarity threshold
        filtered_results = df_embeddings[df_embeddings['similarity'] > similarity_cutoff]

        if filtered_results.empty:
            logging.warning("No relevant content found.")
            return "No relevant content found."

        # Select top matching results
        top_matches = filtered_results.nlargest(max_results, 'similarity')

        # Format the retrieved snippets for AI model input
        formatted_snippets = "\n\n".join(
            f"Relevant Snippet {i+1}: {row['content']}" 
            for i, (_, row) in enumerate(top_matches.iterrows())
        )

        return formatted_snippets

    except Exception as e:
        logging.error(f"Error in retrieving relevant text chunks: {e}")
        return "No relevant content found."

To retrieve relevant information for each company based on the predefined queries, we iterate over the list of companies and use their corresponding pre-calculated embeddings. For each query, we identify and store the most relevant text chunks from the company's document corpus. This process ensures that each company’s data is enriched with contextually relevant insights, making it ready for further analysis or model input.

In [42]:
# Dictionary to store retrieved data for each company and each query
company_retrieved_data = {}

# Retrieve the pre-calculated embeddings for the current company
company_embeddings = company_embeddings_dict.get(company_name)

if company_embeddings is not None:
    # Initialize a dictionary to store the results for the current company
    company_retrieved_data[company_name] = {}

    # Iterate over each query and retrieve relevant information for the company
    for query_text in queries:
        # Retrieve the relevant text chunks for the current query
        retrieved_information = retrieve_top_matching_chunks(company_embeddings, query_text)
        company_retrieved_data[company_name][query_text] = retrieved_information
else:
    # Log if no embeddings were found for the company
    logging.warning(f"No embeddings found for company '{company_name}'.")

To verify the correctness and relevance of the retrieved information, we can perform a check for a specific company and query. This step ensures that the data retrieval process is functioning as expected and provides an example of the most relevant context for a given company-query combination.

In [43]:
# Specify the company and query for checking correctness
query_to_check = query_solvency_ratio  # Example query (choose from predefined queries)

# Retrieve embeddings for the specified company
company_embeddings_to_check = company_embeddings_dict.get(company_name)

if company_embeddings_to_check is not None:
    # Retrieve the relevant data for the company
    company_retrieved_data_for_check = company_retrieved_data.get(company_name, {})

    if company_retrieved_data_for_check:
        # Check if the specific query exists in the retrieved data for the company
        if query_to_check in company_retrieved_data_for_check:
            retrieved_info = company_retrieved_data_for_check[query_to_check]
            
            # Output the most relevant context for the specified company and query
            print(f"Example result for company '{company_name}' and query '{query_to_check}':")
            print(retrieved_info)
        else:
            print(f"No retrieved data found for the query '{query_to_check}' for company '{company_name}'.")
    else:
        print(f"No retrieved data found for company '{company_name}'.")
else:
    print(f"No embeddings data found for company '{company_name}'.")

Example result for company 'Generali' and query '
Extract the group's solvency capital ratio in percentage for 2024, along with the regulatory framework (Solvency II or SST).
':
Relevant Snippet 1: he definitions of the SII regime. The ratio has to be intended 
as preliminary since the definitive Regulatory Solvency Ratio will 
be submitted to the supervisory authority in accordance with 
the timing provided by the Solvency II regulations for the official 
reporting.
For interim reporting, the Own Funds are reported net of accrued 
pro-rata dividend: the disclosed Solvency Ratio, therefore, differs 
from the regulatory view that, from 2024, requires the deduction 
of the full-year dividend also for interim QRT regulatory reporting.
453
Annual Integrated Report and Consolidated Financial Statements 2024
454
Generali Group
Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): 17 objectives 
contained in the 2030 Agenda for sustainable development, 
launched by the United Nations.
Taxonomy-aligned economic activity: an economic activity 
that is described in Annexes I and II of the Commission 
Delegated Regulation EU 2021/2139 of 4 June 2021 (known 
as Taxonomy Climate Delegated Regulation) adopted pursuant 
to Regulation EU 2020/852 and that meets all of the technical 
screening criteria laid down in those Annexes.
Taxonomy-eligible economic activity: an economic activity 
that is described in Annexes I and II of the Commission 
Delegated Regulation EU 2021/2139 of 4 June 2021 (known as 
Taxonomy Climate Delegated Regulation) adopted pursuant to 
Regulation EU 2020/852 irrespective of whether that economic 
activity meets any or all of the technical screening criteria laid 
down in those Annexes.
Third-Party Assets Under Management (TP AUM): assets 
managed by the Group on behalf of its institutional and retail 
clients, and of insurance companies and pension funds. These 
assets are held off the balance sheet.
Turnover: it refers to the volume of revenues generated during 
the reference year by the businesses in which the Group 
operates. It includes: gross written premiums, income from 
service activity, and Asset Management operating income.
Undertakings not obliged to publi

Relevant Snippet 2: ers and evaluate the impact of a wide range of shocks. 
For this purpose, several sensitivity analyses have been performed as at 31 December 2024, in particular:
• increase and decrease of interest rates by 50 bps;
• increase of Euro area government bonds spread by 50 bps16;
• increase of corporate bonds spread by 50 bps;
• increase and decrease of equity values by 25%.
The changes in terms of percentage points with respect to baseline scenario as at 31 December 2024 (Solvency Ratio equal to 
210%) are the following:

+6 p.p.

Based on last available information for 2024.
Finally, the revision of the assumptions implemented by EIOPA in 2025 for the determination of risk-free rates (primarily relevant for 
China, where the UFR decreases by -15 bps and the Credit Risk Adjustment (CRA) increases by -18 bps) is estimated to have a very 
limited impact on the Group’s Solvency Ratio (-1 p.p.).
Group Partial Internal Model (Group PIM)17
The IM is considered to be the most appropriate way of assessing the Group risk profile. It represents the best way of capturing the risk 
profile of the entire Group and of the legal entities in scope in terms of granularity, calibration and correlation of the various risk factors.
The IM is structured around a Risk Map, defined in the Risk Management Group Policy, which contains all quantifiable risks that 
Generali Group has identified as relevant to its business, allowing for the calculation of the SCR both at single risk level and at higher 
aggregation level.
15. The eligible amount of Tier 1 items shall be at least 80% of the MCR; the same limitation on subordinated liabilities and preference shares applied for the SCR coverage is set also for MCR coverage. The eligible amounts of 
Tier 2 items shall not

Relevant Snippet 3: e to (i) provide a comprehensive risk reporting and of the overall solvency needs 
on a current and forward looking basis and of (ii) supporting the Group risk strategy update. In addition to ORSA process, the Group 
also relies on a set of tools, such as the Recovery Plan, the Liquidity Risk Management Plan and the Systemic Risk Management 
Plan defined following the Financial Stability Board (FSB) and the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) standards.
In this context, the Group has identified several risk profiles (e.g. Life Underwriting risk, P&C underwriting risk, financial and credit 
risks) that are managed, mitigated and monitored with reference to capital requirement.
Considering all the above, the following section reports the sensitivity analysis of the Shareholders’ equity attributable to the Group, 
the consolidated result of the period attributable to the Group, and the Life CSM under scenarios considered relevant for market and 
insurance risk. The analysis, which required complex and judgmental valuations, considers the theoretical impact of an instantaneous 
shock at the end of the reporting period (i.e., 31/12/2024) on the value of investments and insurance liabilities. Furthermore, it does 
not take into account any direct or indirect mitigation actions or cross-effects among stressed variables. Therefore, the different 
impacts shown should not be interpreted as representative of the cumulative effects of the different scenarios illustrated.
344
Annual Integrated Report and Consolidated Financial Statements 2024
Generali Group
The following table shows the percentage change of the base amount for each respective stress applied. Moreover, with reference 
to CSM, the percentages are derived as ratio between the impact of the scenario on the CSM after release, based on coverage units 
related to last reporting period, and the value of Life CSM gross of reinsurance as of 31st December 2024.
Sensitivity analysis
(€ million)
31/12/

Relevant Snippet 4: n a current and forward-
looking basis. The ORSA process ensures an ongoing assessment of the solvency position based on the Strategic Plan and the 
Group Capital Management Plan. 
The Group ORSA Report, documenting main results of this process, is produced on an annual basis. A non-regular ORSA Report 
can also be produced in case of significant changes of the risk profile.
Annual Integrated Report and Consolidated Financial Statements 2024
184
Generali Group
C. SOLVENCY POSITION 
Solvency Capital Requirement Coverage
Risk and capital management are closely integrated processes aimed at managing the Group solvency position (or capital position) 
and the Group risk profile. 
The solvency position is defined as the ratio between Group Own Funds (GOF) and Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR).
As at year-end 2024, the solvency position of the Group is confirmed solid, with the Solvency Ratio at 210% (220% as at 31 
December 2023). The robust contribution of the normalized capital generation has partially offset the effects of M&A operations 
(primarily driven by the acquisition of Liberty Seguros), economic variances (mainly due to the widening of non-domestic government 
bond spreads and declining interest rates, partially mitigated by positive equity market performance), non-economic variances 
(reflecting the trend on lapses in Italy and France and the update of related assumptions, and non-operating holding and other 
expenses, mostly compensated by derivative positions and derisking activities on the equity market), regulatory changes (largely 
attributable to the changes introduced by EIOPA at the beginning of the year and the ineligibility of subordinated debt transferred 
from Genertel to Assicurazioni Generali), and capital movements (stemming from the impact of the foreseeable dividend of the 
period9 and the share buyback programme, net of the subordinated debt issuance completed in the last quarter).
SCR coverage

31/12/2024
31/12/2023

• Tier 3 is composed by net deferred tax assets, which are characterised by lower capital quality being not immediately available to 
absorb losses.

For 2024, Group Own Funds confirmed the high-quality of the capital tiering. Tier 1 amounts to 84.2% of the total (85.6% in 2023), 
Tier 2 represents 15.4% (13.9% in 2023), and Tier 3 only 0.4% of the total (as at year-end 2023). 
No eligibility deductions are triggered thanks to the high-quality of the capital-tiering.
12. These items were issued before the entry into force of the Solvency II Directive and cover the Solvency margin up to 50% according to Solvency I regime.
13. Differently from Tier 1 restricted, these grandfathered items cover the Solvency margin up to 25% according to Solvency I regime.
